term: What is the professional engineer's mandatory ethical duty when encountering a design aspect that falls outside their specific area of training, knowledge, or experience?
definition:
The engineer must immediately cease work on that specific portion and consult a qualified specialist in that discipline (e.g., another Professional Engineer or specialized Geo) to perform that work. The specialist must take direct supervision and control of that portion and apply their own professional seal.

This concept is rooted in the Professional Engineers Act and Code of Ethics, which places Public Safety as the paramount duty. Undertaking work beyond competence (even with good intentions) is negligence. The standard is absolute: you must know your limitations. If you proceed anyway, you violate your professional standards. Consulting a specialist does not absolve you of overall project responsibility, but it ensures technical accuracy and the correct professional seal for that discipline's work.

term: Cost model for official electronic seals
definition: Expect a one-time setup fee plus annual renewal to the certified digital-signature provider.

term: First step for suspected falsified test results
definition: Discuss concerns with the colleague; if not resolved, escalate to employer and regulator.

term: Obligation when reviewing another professional's work
definition: Inform the original professional of the review before starting, even if the employer requested it.

term: What should you do if a project goes beyond your competence?
definition:
Seek qualified assistance immediately; do not delay the client's project while you retrain (NPPE III.3).

If a job goes beyond your skills, you must get qualified help right away.
Don’t try to handle it alone or delay the client’s work while you retrain.
The priority is protecting the client and doing the work properly and safely.

Example

An engineer is asked to design a complex electrical system but only has experience with mechanical design.
Instead of trying to figure it out slowly or saying yes and hoping for the best, they call in an electrical expert.
This keeps the project moving and avoids mistakes.
The engineer can learn more later, but the client’s project must not suffer in the meantime.

term: Peer sealing work they did not supervise
definition: Yes - if they perform a thorough review and accept full legal responsibility, they may sign & seal (NPPE III.3, Seal guideline).

term: Research scientist vs. Engineer core roles
definition: Research scientist generates new knowledge; engineer applies that knowledge (NPPE III.3).

term: Natural greenhouse effect importance
definition: It's essential for life on Earth—retains heat and prevents extreme temperature swings (NPPE III.6).

term: Required environmental literacy for P.Eng./P.Geo.
definition: Possess enough knowledge to judge when a specialist is needed; not expected to be an environmental expert in all areas (Env. Guideline §3.1).

term: Meaning of "peak oil"
definition: After peak, easy oil is gone ⇒ production declines & prices likely rise sharply (NPPE III.6).

term: Causal chain to climate change
definition: 1️⃣ Gas emissions → 2️⃣ Enhanced greenhouse effect → 3️⃣ Global warming → 4️⃣ Climate change (NPPE III.6).

term: Duty that violates your morals but not law/Ethics Code
definition: You may refuse, yet employer is entitled to discipline or dismiss you (Conscience vs. employment, NPPE III.2).

term: Who protects the owner's interests on a project?
definition: The consultant (engineer/geo) guides the client with technical advice and safeguards fairness, safety & schedule (Client-consultant duty, NPPE III.2).

term: Duty of loyalty/confidentiality in private practice
definition: A self-employed P.Eng./P.Geo. owes the same loyalty & confidentiality to clients that an employee owes to an employer.

term: "Duty to inform" when client overrules advice
definition: Must clearly disclose risks—especially when the client lacks technical expertise—before accepting overridden direction.

term: Risk-management duty in the Code
definition: Professionals must recognize, eliminate or reduce hazards that could harm people or property.

term: QA trigger for design software
definition: Perform quality-assurance every time the software is updated, re-configured, or newly installed.

term:
What's the biggest myth about deleting emails?
myth : AFSANE
definition: That they're gone forever. In reality, emails are almost always recoverable—making them a high liability risk if you assume they can simply be deleted.

term: Are insurers a direct accountability stakeholder?
definition:
No - professionals are influenced by insurers but not directly accountable to them (unlike employers, clients, public, courts).
In a very simple way, this means, Insurance companies can affect your decisions
But they are not your boss in a legal or professional duty sense



Example:

A civil engineer is designing a bridge.
They are directly accountable to the client, the public, and engineering regulators.
Their insurance company may require safety procedures to reduce risk.
The engineer may follow those insurance rules to keep coverage valid.
However, the insurer does not have direct authority over the engineer’s professional duty.
The engineer is accountable to safety laws and clients, not the insurer itself.

term: Posting false statements online
definition: Internet libel is an actionable tort; same liability as other media.

term: Coverage of a Commercial General Liability (CGL) policy
definition: Third-party bodily injury and property damage claims.

term: Working abroad with weak safety rules
definition: Must still follow Canadian Code-of-Ethics duty: improve conditions even if local laws are silent/weak.

term: What exactly is a "dummy run" (or sanity check) when you adopt new engineering software?
definition: Run a simple test case with a known answer to confirm the program's basic computations before relying on it.

term: You’re sued over an alleged design error. Which insurance nickname covers this kind of professional negligence?
definition: Errors & Omissions (E&O) insurance — i.e., professional liability.

term: What does McGregor’s Theory Y assume about employees?
definition: People naturally want to work; with purpose, trust, and autonomy, they’ll self-direct and can exceed expectations.

term: Why is stamping a 66 % (preliminary) completed drawings wrong?
definition: Only final professional work products may be sealed. Preliminary sets must remain un-stamped.

term: ISO 9000 vs. ISO 14000
definition: ISO 9000 → Quality-management systems. ISO 14000 → Environmental-management systems.

term: Recommending a relative for a contract - neutralising conflict of interest
definition: Fully disclose the relationship to the client/employer before they act on the recommendation.

term: Standards vs. codes
definition: Standards primarily guarantee quality & uniformity of materials/processes; they do not dictate aesthetic sameness.

term: 1989 Alaskan disaster that inspired the CERES sustainability principles
definition: Exxon Valdez oil-spill.

term: Duty of loyalty/confidentiality for sole-practitioner consultants
definition: They owe that duty to their clients, just as employees owe it to their employer.

term: Who legally owns your physical or digital professional seal?
definition: Your provincial/territorial association; you must surrender it on demand.

term: Forgot to stamp a final drawing but you prepared/reviewed it - liability status
definition: Still liable. Lack of a seal doesn't erase your professional or legal responsibility.

term: What is a low-cost method to independently verify the accuracy of engineering software output for a project?
definition:
Perform at least one hand calculation on a simplified case and compare it to the software output to ensure accuracy.

Example: For a bridge design, manually calculate the stress on a single beam under a simplified load and check if it matches the software's result. (Syllabus topic I.3, Ethics 5th ed. p.39 or 6th ed. p.35)

term: May you bill a 'stamp fee' on top of your review time?
definition: No. You can invoice for your professional hours, but charging for the mere act of stamping is prohibited.

term: Common misconception about email deletion and legal discovery
definition: Email is almost never truly deleted; it can usually be recovered in investigations — high liability risk.

term: Key traits of an effective Communications-Systems Policy (CSP)
definition: Must be written, legally vetted, and educate users on risks; a purely verbal policy is inadequate.

term: Quick definition: what makes a dummy run different from reading the manual?
definition: Manual = understanding usage; dummy run = executing a test case with known results inside the software.

term: Client presses for drawings
definition: Tell the client it's a top priority but release the drawings only after a full, careful review.

term: The Single Most Important Rule of the Canada Fisheries Act (PENG Exam)
definition:
The Habitat-Protection Clause (HADD Rule)

Quick Summary: It FORBIDS any activity that harms fish habitat, even if no fish are killed directly.

PENG Context (Section 35): As an engineer, you must prevent HADD (Harmful Alteration, Disruption or Destruction) of habitat. If your project risks HADD, you need DFO authorization first.

term: Director who owns shares in a bidder
definition: What must you do? Declare the conflict and abstain from the vote.

term: Qualifications-Based Selection (QBS)
definition: How is the consultant's detailed scope of services produced? Client and consultant develop the scope jointly.

term: Software-verification term
definition: Run a simple test case with a known answer to see if the code behaves. Dummy run / sanity check.

term: According to McGregor's management theory Y, what are the fundamental assumptions of a Theory Y manager regarding employees and work?
definition:
Employees naturally like to work and, if properly motivated, will exceed expectations.

1. Employees find work as natural as rest or play (they inherently like it).
2. If properly motivated, they will exercise self-direction.
3. If committed to goals, they will exceed expectations (not just meet minimums).
The input establishes that Theory Y sees work enjoyment as natural and intrinsic. This is the foundation: people *want* to be productive.

term: Three Code-of-Ethics options when employer's order endangers the public
definition:
(1) Correct the problem (2) Whistle-blow (3) Resign in protest.

Simplified Explanation:

When a worker is told to do something that could harm the public, ethical rules suggest three main choices. First, they can try to correct the problem by speaking up or fixing the issue internally. Second, they can whistle-blow, meaning they report the danger to outside authorities. Third, they can resign in protest, which means quitting the job because they disagree with the unsafe order. These options help protect public safety and maintain ethical responsibility.

Example:

An engineer is told to approve a building design they believe is unsafe. First, they try to correct the issue by warning their manager. If nothing changes, they may report it to regulators (whistle-blow). If the problem is still ignored, they might decide to resign in protest. Each option is a way to avoid supporting something that could harm the public.

term: Why is it wrong to seal "66 % complete" design drawings?
definition: Only final professional work products may be sealed; preliminaries stay un-stamped.

term:
Collegial (Theory Y) management style: How do staff operate, and what is the manager's role?

("Collegial" just means "cooperative and friendly, like colleagues working together as equals," not anything to do with college classes. It comes from the same root as "colleague," meaning peers, rather than from "college."
Ask ChatGPT)
definition:
Staff work autonomously within an agreed framework; the manager acts as a facilitator—providing guidance, resources and removing obstacles—rather than micromanaging each task.

Example: A design team defines its own milestones and divides tasks among themselves; the manager checks in to offer support or training but doesn't assign or oversee every detail.

term: Accepting a job with a client's competitor
definition: New contract must not require disclosure of former client's confidential affairs.

term: Recommending your brother-in-law for paid work
definition: What must you do first? Fully disclose the relationship to the client to manage the conflict of interest.

term: Designing safely when codes are missing
definition: Deviate from or exceed the code using accepted engineering principles to protect the public.

term: Primary purpose of technical standards
definition: To guarantee quality and uniformity of materials, products, or processes.

term: What global climate pact was adopted in 2015, and what is its temperature goal?
definition: The Paris Agreement; it aims to keep global warming “well below 2°C” above pre-industrial levels.

term: When must design software be re-validated/quality-checked?
definition: Every time it is updated, patched, or re-configured.

term: What does a Commercial General Liability (CGL) policy cover for engineering businesses?
definition:
Third-party bodily injury (BI) and property damage (PD).

Commercial General Liability (CGL)
A type of insurance that protects businesses if someone else gets hurt or their property is damaged because of the business’s operations.

A CGL policy helps engineering businesses if someone outside the company gets hurt or has their property damaged because of the company’s work. It doesn’t cover damage to the company’s own things—just problems caused to others. This is important because it protects the business from lawsuits and big bills if accidents happen during a project or at the office.

Imagine an engineering company is visiting a client’s building, and one of their tools falls and injures someone—that’s bodily injury. Or maybe they accidentally damage a server room, causing expensive problems—property damage. In both cases, the CGL insurance would help pay for legal costs or compensation. That way, the business doesn't have to pay out of pocket.

term: Indigenous-reserve projects: why can't you finance by mortgage?
definition: Mortgages can't be registered on reserve land because the land is generally unpatented (Crown/Indigenous ownership).

term: Canadian law that governs how private-sector firms collect, use & disclose personal data
definition: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act.

term: When can an engineer/geoscientist file a construction lien?
definition:
Allowed if the engineer/geoscientist is a prime consultant (not merely a sub-consultant).

An engineer or geoscientist can file a lien to claim unpaid money only if they were hired as the main consultant (called a prime consultant). If they worked under someone else (like a sub-consultant), they can’t file a lien. The right to file a lien is for those who are directly responsible to the client. This helps protect primary contractors, not every person involved.

Example:

Jane is an engineer hired directly by a building owner to manage the whole project. If she doesn’t get paid, she can file a lien on the property to get her money. But Sam, who worked under Jane doing only part of the work, cannot file a lien himself. Only Jane has that legal right, since she is the prime consultant.

term: Must money always change hands to form a contract?
definition: No. Consideration can be anything of value, not necessarily cash.

term: Sexual-harassment cases are heard where, and how broadly is harassment defined?
definition:
Human-rights tribunal; takes a wide view - unwelcome comments or advances suffice, no physical assault needed.
Simplified Explanation:
Sexual-harassment cases are usually handled by a human-rights tribunal. These tribunals take a broad view of what counts as harassment. This means even unwanted comments or advances can be enough. It does not require physical contact or assault. The focus is on whether the behavior was unwelcome and inappropriate.

Example:
A worker keeps making inappropriate jokes to a coworker. The coworker feels uncomfortable and asks them to stop. Even if there is no physical contact, this can still be harassment. The case can be taken to a human-rights tribunal. The tribunal looks at whether the behavior was unwanted. If it was, action can be taken.

term: What offence involves buying shares based on confidential test results, and under which law is it prohibited?
definition: Insider trading - illegal under the Criminal Code of Canada, section 382.1.

term: What does federal law require when a project may affect Indigenous rights on claimed land?
definition:
Duty rests on the Crown (government) for any project on claimed land, proven or unproven.

Example

Let’s say the government wants to build a pipeline through a forest. An Indigenous group says that land is part of their traditional territory, even if no court has officially confirmed it yet. The government still has a duty to talk with them, listen to their concerns, and possibly change the plan to reduce harm. That’s consultation and accommodation, and it's the government’s job, not the company’s.

term: Accurate description of force majeure clause
definition: Excuses delay for events beyond parties' control (e.g., strikes, fires, pandemics).

term: What must a consultant and client do under Qualifications-Based Selection (QBS) for scope?
definition:
Jointly develop the detailed scope of services after selection.

Simplified Explanation:

Under Qualifications-Based Selection (QBS), the consultant and client work together to define the scope of services. This happens after the consultant is selected, based on their qualifications. The “scope of services” means a clear list of what work will be done and how it will be done. Both sides agree on the details together. This ensures the project is well planned before work starts.

Example:

A city hires an engineering consultant using QBS. First, they choose the best-qualified expert. After that, the city and the consultant meet to plan the project. They decide exactly what tasks the consultant will do, like designing a bridge and doing safety checks. This agreed plan becomes the scope of services.

term: Subsurface anchors installed without consent on a neighbour's land
definition:
Is it trespass? Yes. Property rights extend below and above the surface; unauthorized encroachment is trespass even if harmless.

Yes, it is trespass if you install things like anchors underground on someone else’s land without their permission. Property rights don’t just cover the surface—they go above and below the ground too. Even if the anchor doesn’t cause any damage, it’s still illegal. The law says unauthorized use is trespassing, no matter how harmless it seems. You always need the owner’s consent.

Example:

A contractor installs anchors underground that extend into a neighbour’s property. The anchors are hidden and don’t cause any harm. But the neighbour didn’t agree to it. Even though nothing is damaged, the contractor has trespassed. The neighbour could legally demand removal or sue for it.

term: In Canadian tort law, what is a professional’s “duty to warn”?
definition: An obligation to warn about impending damage or injury to people or property when aware (or reasonably foreseeable). It applies even if the hazard isn’t in your scope—you must take steps to warn/prevent harm.

term: For liability in tort, the defendant's act must be ___ or ___
definition: Intentional or negligent.

term: Under the Canadian Environmental Protection Act (CEPA 1999), what level of enforcement power do CEPA officers hold?
definition:
The Rule: CEPA officers have enforcement powers COMPARABLE TO POLICE. 

The Trap: Do not assume environmental officers only hand out minor fines or administrative warnings. They have the authority to investigate, conduct searches, seize property, and lay criminal charges. They do NOT have "significantly less" power than regular police.

term: What does "privileged" mean for statements made during negotiation?
definition: They cannot be used as evidence in any later court or arbitration proceedings.

term: Is different pay for the same job always discrimination?
definition: No - employers may differentiate on legitimate grounds such as greater experience or tenure; human-rights statutes bar discrimination only on protected grounds (sex, race, age, etc.).

term: Two clauses every multi-jurisdiction contract needs to avoid a conflict-of-laws fight
definition: • Governing-law clause (which jurisdiction's law applies) • Forum/venue clause (which courts will hear any dispute).

term: Negligence, contracts, insurance - similarity of rules across Canada's common- & civil-law systems
definition: Basic principles are fairly consistent in both systems; differences seldom affect normal engineering practice.

term: Proper role of an expert witness
definition: Give neutral, independent technical opinion - not act as advocate for either party.

term:
In the case citation “2 S.C.R. 547 (1998),” what does the “2” mean?

(Case citation means A standard legal reference to help people find a court decision.
S.C.R. Short for Supreme Court Reports — the official record of decisions by Canada’s Supreme Court.)
definition: It’s the volume number of the Supreme Court Reports (not a chapter).

term:
Why is construction litigation often slow and costly?

Litigation: the process of resolving disputes in court
Specs: short for "specifications," the detailed description of work and materials
definition:
Because it typically involves many stakeholders (owners, general contractors, subcontractors, suppliers) plus massive volumes of technical records—drawings, specs (detailed requirements), emails—that lawyers must painstakingly review.

Example:A delay dispute on a high-rise can drag on 12 months when counsel from the owner, GC and three subs pore over 10,000 emails and 800 CAD drawings—each page adding hours of billable time.

term: Which court handles cases related to the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms?
definition:
The Supreme Court of Canada.

Here is the relation between the Charter and the Constitution:
The Charter is the part of the Constitution that protects your basic rights.

Think of the Constitution as the foundation of the whole Canadian legal house.
Inside that house, the Charter is the room where all your rights and freedoms live.

term: Harris, Pritchard & Rabins 'Golden Rule' for engineers abroad
definition:
Professionals should not impose their own choices on local people.

Simplified Explanation:

The Golden Rule here means a basic guideline for how engineers should behave when working in other countries. It says that professionals should not force their own ideas or decisions on the local community. Instead, they should respect what local people want and need. The focus is on respect, understanding, and cooperation. Engineers should work with people, not over them.

Example:

An engineer from another country goes to help build a water system in a village. Instead of deciding everything alone, they ask the villagers what they prefer. The villagers might want a system that fits their daily habits. The engineer listens and adjusts the plan. This way, the project works better for everyone. It shows respect for the local community.

term: Liability of Partners in a General Partnership-What liability do partners face for partnership debts and wrongful acts?
definition:
▪️ Definition: In a general partnership, all partners are jointly and severally liable for the firm's debts and any wrongful acts, even if they didn't cause them.
▪️ Meaning of "jointly and severally": Each partner can be held responsible both together (jointly) and on their own (severally) for the entire obligation.
▪️ Example: The partnership owes a supplier $50 000. The supplier can sue Partner A alone for the full $50 000, or any other partner, regardless of who actually ordered the goods.

term: Negligence, contracts, insurance across Canada's systems
definition: Basic principles are fairly consistent in both systems; differences seldom affect normal engineering practice.

term: Quebec Civil Code on contract interpretation
definition: Code says common intention prevails over literal text.

term: Three elements of Canada's Constitution
definition: 1) Division of powers between federal & provincial governments 2) Creation of the courts 3) Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.

term: Duty of every worker under Occupational Health & Safety Acts
definition: To take reasonable care to protect the health and safety of co-workers and anyone nearby while the worker is working.

term: Trade-name vs. trademark
definition: A trade name is only a provincial business registration.

term: What is mediator confidentiality in dispute resolution?
definition:
Definition: All communications (written or spoken) shared with the mediator during a mediation are legally privileged, meaning they cannot be disclosed or used later in arbitration or court.
Key term: Privileged - protected by law from being revealed as evidence.
Example: In private caucus, Company A admits it overlooked a safety risk. That admission stays in mediation and can't be brought into a later lawsuit.

term: What is the meaning of 10% bid bond?
definition:
Guarantees that the winning bidder will enter into the contract and supply performance security; if the bidder backs out, the surety pays the owner up to the bond amount (typically 10% of the bid).

Simplified Explanation:

A 10% bid bond is like a financial promise that a company is serious about a job they are trying to win. It ensures that if the company wins the contract, they will actually follow through and provide extra guarantees to do the work. If they win but then refuse to start the job, the surety (guarantee company) pays the project owner some money. That payment can be up to 10% of the bid amount. It protects the project owner from losing time and money if the bidder backs out.

Example:

A construction company bids $1,000,000 to build a school.
They include a 10% bid bond, which equals $100,000.
If they win the contract but decide not to do the job, the surety steps in.
The surety pays the school district up to $100,000 as compensation.
This helps the school district cover delays or find another contractor.

term: TN entry for Canadian engineer
definition: Proof of Canadian or Mexican citizenship + a letter or contract describing the professional purpose of entry.

term: What is Governing-law clause?
definition:
Definition: A governing-law clause is a part of a contract that tells which state or country's laws will be used to decide any arguments about the deal. It helps everyone know in advance where to go and what rules to follow if they disagree. By choosing a specific jurisdiction, the parties reduce surprises and legal costs. This clause appears near the end of most agreements. It's especially important in deals crossing borders.
Key term: Jurisdiction → the legal authority or region whose courts and laws apply.
Example: "Imagine two companies—one in California and one in Germany—signing a sales contract. They include a governing-law clause saying California law will apply. Later, they disagree over payment terms. Both sides then know they must ask a California court to interpret the contract. This saves time debating which country's rules to use. It makes the legal process clearer and faster."
Persian (Farsi): BANDE GHANOON-E HAKEM!

term: Forum/venue clause
definition:
States which courts will hear any dispute in multi-country deals.

Phrase	and their Simple Meanings:
Forum/venue clause means A contract term that says where legal disputes will be handled.
Dispute	means A disagreement or legal conflict.
Multi-country deals means Agreements involving parties from different countries.
Courts means The legal system/judges that decide cases.

Simplified Explanation:
A forum/venue clause is a part of a contract that chooses which court or which country’s courts will handle disagreements. This is important in international deals, where companies may be in different countries. It helps avoid confusion about where a lawsuit should be filed. It can also reduce legal fights over jurisdiction. In short, it sets the location for resolving disputes.

Example:
A U.S. company and a German company sign a contract. The contract says any dispute must be heard in New York courts. If a disagreement happens, both sides must go there. They cannot argue later about using German courts instead. That is how a forum/venue clause works.

term: What is a no-damages-for-delay clause in a construction contract?
definition:
Blocks a contractor from claiming extra "damages" (compensation) for delays they cause, but doesn't stop claims if the owner orders a shutdown.

• Example: If a crew miss-schedule causes a week's delay, the contractor gets no extra pay. But if the owner suspends work for permits, the contractor can recover added costs.

Simplified Explanation:

A no-damages-for-delay clause means a contractor usually cannot ask for extra money when delays happen, even if the delay increases costs. It protects the owner from paying for many delay-related claims. If the delay is caused by the contractor’s own scheduling problems, they usually absorb those losses. However, this clause often may not apply when the owner causes the delay, such as ordering a work shutdown. In that case, the contractor may be able to claim extra costs. The exact result depends on the contract wording and local law.

Example:

A contractor plans to pour concrete on Monday, but poor scheduling delays the crew a week. Because of a no-damages-for-delay clause, the contractor may not get extra pay for that delay. But if the owner stops work while waiting for permits, the contractor may seek payment for added labor or equipment costs. In short, contractor-caused delays often are not compensated, while owner-caused delays may be treated differently.

term: What is a Per-Diem contract rate, and when is it the best choice to use?
definition:
Definition: A fixed, flat daily fee paid to a consultant ("per day").

When to use it: When the project scope or schedule is unpredictable. It provides daily cost certainty without needing exact hourly estimates.

Example: Paying a site inspector $800/day. You pay the same flat rate whether they actively inspect for 4 hours or troubleshoot for 10 hours that day.

term: What is quantum meruit and when can you use it?
definition:
Definition: Latin for "as much as deserved." Allows you to recover a reasonable value for services and materials when no formal contract exists but the work was accepted.
Example: You clear a flooded basement in an emergency without a contract; the homeowner benefits and refuses to pay—so you sue on quantum meruit to get paid fairly.

term: Occupational Health & Safety Act
definition: Employers must provide — and workers must help maintain — a safe, healthy workplace.

term: What is the standard term of copyright protection in Canada?
definition: Copyright lasts for the life of the creator plus 70 years.

term: Human Rights Code
definition: Statute that bans hiring discrimination based on disability (or age, religion, etc.).

term: Loser-Pays Cost Rule-What is it, and why does it matter?
definition:
▪️ Definition: A rule in many legal systems where the party that loses a lawsuit must pay a portion (or all) of the winning party's legal costs.
▪️ Purpose: Deters frivolous or weak claims by adding a financial consequence for the loser.
▪️ Example:Acme Corp sues Beta Ltd over a minor contract ambiguity. The court finds in Beta's favor and orders Acme to pay Beta's legal fees. Acme thinks twice before filing similar baseless suits in the future.

term: Workers'-comp premiums
definition:
Calculated per employee and adjusted for the risk level of the employer's industry.

Simplified Explanation:

This means that a company pays insurance costs for each employee. The amount depends on how many workers there are and how risky their jobs are. If the job is more dangerous, the company pays more money. If it’s safer, they pay less. The type of industry (like construction vs. office work) affects the price.

Example:

A construction company has workers who use heavy machines and work at heights. Because this job is riskier, their insurance cost per worker is higher. An office company has employees who sit at desks most of the day. Since this job is safer, their insurance cost is lower. So, the total cost depends on both the number of workers and how risky their jobs are.

term: Arbitration in construction contracts
definition: Construction contracts often use arbitration instead of court because it is generally faster, cheaper, and private compared with litigation.

term: Entering the U.S. as a Canadian engineer under CUSMA/USMCA
definition: Proof of Canadian citizenship + a letter or contract stating the professional purpose (TN category).

term: What is a change order?
definition:
A change order is basically a small written update to the contract when something changes during the project.
Think of it as a receipt that says:

how much extra work is being added

or how much work is being removed (a credit)

and what the new total contract price becomes.

It’s simple, but important. Without a change order, arguments explode later about “who agreed to what.”
So it acts like a financial bookmark that keeps everyone honest.

Analogy:
Imagine you’re at a barbershop.
You ask for a haircut ($30), but halfway through you say, “Can you also trim my beard?” The barber says, “That’s $10 extra.”
A change order is the written note that says:
“Haircut $30 + Beard trim $10 = $40 total.”
No surprises. No fights.

term: Key difference: Québec Civil Code vs. Common-law courts
definition: Civil Code puts less weight on case precedent; Common Law relies on it heavily.

term: Five things that make a contract enforceable
definition: Offer/acceptance, mutual intent, consideration, capacity, and a lawful purpose (the deal itself can't be illegal).

term: Legal systems used in Canada
definition: Common law (everywhere except Québec) and Civil law (Québec).

term:
What's the golden rule for expert witness testimony?
(Expert witness = a specialist who gives opinion evidence in court)
definition:
Only offer opinions on matters within your documented expertise.

• E.g., a geotechnical engineer shouldn't testify on electrical circuitry.

term: CCDC 2
definition:
A standard Canadian fixed-price construction contract form issued by the Canadian Construction Documents Committee.

Example: A municipality uses CCDC 2 to award a $5 million bid for its new library, locking in the price and scope agreed in the contract.

term: What is a material mistake in contract law?
definition:
A material mistake is a significant error about a fact central to the contract that existed when the agreement was made, allowing the mistaken party to void the contract.

Example: Alice buys what she believes is an original painting. After signing, she learns it's a cheap replica. Because authenticity was essential, she can cancel the deal.

Difficult word:
Voidable - able to be legally canceled or undone by one party.

term: Doctrine of Estoppel-What stops a party from enforcing strict contract terms after they've let the other side ignore them?
definition:
A legal rule that prevents someone from insisting on a strict contract right if they previously waived it or led the other side to believe they would be lenient.

Example: A landlord accepts late rent for months without penalty. Later, they cannot suddenly enforce a late‐fee for those same missed due dates.

Difficult word:Waive - to voluntarily give up a right.

term: In Canada, what type of automatically acquired trademark right requires no official registration, application fees, or government approval, relying solely on established public use and a recognized business reputation in a specific area?
definition:
A Common-law Trademark Right. It is the most affordable way to secure basic protection for a business name, established through consistent public use in a defined geographical area.

Simplified Explanation:

Common-law trademark rights mean you can get some legal protection for a business name just by using it in real life, even if you don’t officially register it. If you use a name consistently and publicly, you may gain rights to it in your area. This is often the cheapest way to protect a business name in Canada. However, the protection is usually stronger if you register it officially. It depends on how well-known and widely used the name is.

Example:

A person starts a bakery called “Sunny Bakes” and uses that name in ads, signs, and sales across several cities. Over time, people recognize the name as theirs. Even without registration, they may gain common-law trademark rights. If another bakery nearby tries to use the same name, they may be able to challenge it. However, their protection is stronger if they officially register the trademark.

term: Duration of Canadian copyright after creator's death
definition: Life of the author plus 70 years.

term: Goal of arbitration versus court on construction jobs
definition: Generally cheaper, faster, and private compared with litigation.

term: What are the five essential elements required for a contract to be legally enforceable in Canada?
definition:
O A M C L → Offer/Acceptance, Mutual intent, Consideration, Capacity, Lawful purpose.

The five elements are: Offer and Acceptance, Mutual Intent to be bound, Consideration (something of value exchanged), Capacity (parties' legal ability to contract), and Lawful Purpose (legal objective).Example: A P.Eng. offers to design a bridge, the client accepts, both intend to be bound, payment is agreed, both are competent, and the purpose is legal. (Syllabus topic I.3, Law text p.12 or p.18)

term: After a formal discipline hearing finds a member guilty, who issues the order of discipline?
definition: The Discipline Committee issues the order of discipline (e.g., fine, suspension, terms).

term: Discipline vs. Enforcement
definition: The association charges a member with incompetence or breaching the Code of Ethics, then holds a hearing.

term: Discipline order effect
definition: The discipline order takes effect immediately; no formal hearing is held if the investigated engineer admits misconduct and accepts the proposed penalty.

term: What is the typical order of a Practice Review Board (PRB) check?
definition: (1) Review the firm’s written practice/quality procedures → (2) meet/interview key staff → (3) issue a report with findings & recommendations.

term: The Primary Purpose of a Firm Practice Review
definition:
Core Objective: To verify that the engineering firm has implemented and actively follows an adequate "practice plan."

Key Tasks:
- Flag risks or gaps in the firm's quality management and ethical practices.
- Ensure compliance with professional standards and regulations.

*What it is NOT*: A practice review is NOT an audit of financial status, profitability, or overall business performance.

term: How does the Practice Review Board (PRB) verify the honesty and accuracy of members' self-reported CPD records each year?
definition: Each year, the Practice Review Board (PRB) audits (officially checks) a random sample of submitted Continuing Professional Development (CPD) records to verify their accuracy and honesty.

term: Engineers Canada's reason for CPD in one word
definition: Maintains competence, a core duty in every Code of Ethics.

term: If two parties to an arbitration can't agree on a single arbitrator, how is the arbitrator chosen?
definition:
Either party may ask the court to appoint the arbitrator.

Arbitrator: a neutral third party who resolves the dispute.

term: Arbitration appointment for low-quality fabric dispute
definition:
A court can appoint the arbitrator upon request if parties picked arbitration but can't agree on an arbitrator.

Simplified Explanation:

If two people agree to solve a problem using arbitration but can’t decide who the arbitrator should be, they can ask a court for help. The court can then choose the arbitrator for them. This usually happens when both sides are stuck and cannot agree. It helps keep the process moving forward. The court does not solve the dispute itself—it only helps pick the person who will.

Example:

Imagine two clothing companies are arguing about low-quality fabric. They agreed earlier to use arbitration instead of going to court. But now, they can’t agree on who should be the arbitrator. One company asks the court for help. The court then appoints an arbitrator. That arbitrator listens to both sides and makes a final decision.

term: Mandatory CPD Programs - What are they and who must do them?
definition:
Continuing Professional Development (CPD) programs mandate ongoing learning (courses, workshops, etc.) to maintain professional competence. They're now required not just for engineers, but also for doctors, lawyers, accountants and others—both across Canada and around the world.

Example: An Ontario engineer must log ≥30 CPD hours/year to renew their P.Eng.; a UK physician must complete 50 CPD credits annually.

term: Personal benefit of keeping up CPD hours
definition: Better chance of promotions or pay-raises - you're more valuable to employers.

term: Reviewer action after failing a practice review
definition: Recommend the member be disciplined (discipline = official penalties from the regulator).

term: Incompetence
definition: Lack of adequate skill or care.

term: PRB (Practice Review Board)
definition: A committee of experienced professionals appointed by the association to audit members' competence and Continuing Professional Development (CPD) records, ensuring they meet practice standards.

term: CPD records audit
definition: To check self-reports are fair and honest - i.e., truthful and complete.

term: Certificate of Authorization / Permit to Practise
definition: Allows the firm to practise engineering / geoscience through employees who are P.Eng./P.Geo. licence-holders.

term: Trigger not used for Association individual Practice Review
definition: "Being internationally educated."

term: Public access to Discipline-Committee hearings
definition: Generally open to the public, closed only if security / confidential matters are involved.

term: Main reason associations audit members' CPD logs
definition: To verify self-reported hours are fair & honest (truthful).

term: Appeal after Practice Review Board (PRB) Decision
definition:
A member may appeal any PRB decision to the Association's Appeal Board, which will re-examine the finding and can confirm, modify, or overturn it.
Appeal Board (a short definition): a committee that reviews and changes (if needed) decisions made by the PRB.

term: Committee membership restriction: Can you serve on both the Investigation (Complaints) Committee and the Discipline Committee?(Investigation Committee - gathers and reviews complaints; Discipline Committee - holds formal hearings)
definition:
No - you may join only one committee at a time, ensuring the investigative and disciplinary stages stay independent and impartial.

You can’t be on both the Investigation and Discipline Committees at the same time.
This rule helps make sure that fact-finding and decision-making stay separate.
The idea is to keep the process fair, with no one person influencing both steps.
You must choose one role—either help investigate or help decide punishment.
It avoids conflict of interest and keeps everything neutral.

Example:

Imagine you're part of a school panel.
If you help gather facts about a cheating case, you shouldn’t also decide the punishment.
Why? Because you might be biased from already seeing the evidence.
So, one group checks the facts, and another group decides what to do.
That’s why you can only join one committee—it keeps things honest and fair.
This way, no one has too much power in the process.

term: Association's power against unlicensed person
definition: Starts an Enforcement process against the non-member (not Discipline, which is only for members).

term: Possible money consequences after misconduct
definition: Member may have to pay the hearing costs and a fine (up to ~$5-15 k, varies by province).

term: Personal benefit of CPD
definition: Better chance of promotions / pay raises - you become more valuable.

term: Three federal environmental statutes
definition: Canadian Environmental Protection Act (CEPA), Fisheries Act, Canadian Environmental Assessment Act (CEAA).

term:
What is the purpose of PIPEDA in Canada, and who does it apply to?

PIPEDA (Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act)
definition:
PIPEDA (Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act) governs how private-sector organizations collect, use, and disclose personal information in Canada, ensuring privacy protection.

Example: An engineering firm must obtain client consent before sharing their personal data with a third party for project purposes. (Syllabus topic I.3, Law text p.12 or p.18)

term: Legally enforceable part of a patent
definition:
The Claims section - it defines the protected features of the invention.

The claims section of a patent is the only part that’s legally protected. It lists the specific features of the invention that others can’t copy. If someone breaks those rules, the inventor can sue them. Other parts of the patent explain things, but only the claims can be enforced in court.

Example:

An inventor patents a new type of water filter. The patent has drawings and descriptions, but the claims section says, “a filter with a triple-layer mesh and a carbon core.” If someone else makes a filter with that same setup, the inventor can take legal action. Only what's in the claims is legally protected.

Alex invents a new type of phone charger and gets a patent. In the Claims section, he describes the exact design and how it works. Another company copies his design. Because it’s covered in his patent claims, Alex can sue them for infringement. The claims define his legal rights.

term: What triggers the requirement for a prime contractor under Canadian Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) regulations for a construction project?
definition:
The requirement for a prime contractor is triggered when two or more employers work simultaneously at a construction site. The prime contractor, designated in the contract, coordinates and ensures the safety of employees and the public. If not specified, the owner assumes this role.

Example: A site with a plumbing and framing contractor requires a prime contractor to manage safety, like ensuring fall protection. (Syllabus topic I.2, Ethics 5th ed. p.146 or 6th ed. p.145)

term: In the context of Occupational Health and Safety (OH&S) legislation, what is the precise definition of an "occupational disease" or "occupational illness"?
definition:
A disease or serious ill-health condition that has a proven and direct causal link to an individual's specific job duties or exposure to specific hazards within their workplace.

Simplified Explanation:

An occupational disease (or illness) is a health problem that is caused by a person’s job or workplace. It happens because of conditions at work, not random or unrelated causes. The illness must be directly linked to the work being done. These rules are set out in occupational health and safety laws. The key idea is that the job itself is what leads to the sickness.

Example:

A factory worker breathes in harmful dust every day at work. Over time, they develop a lung disease. Doctors confirm it is caused by the workplace environment. Since the illness is directly linked to the job, it is considered an occupational disease.

term: Main goal of WHMIS
definition: Give trained workers clear information (labels + SDS) when handling hazardous materials.

term: Unprofessional conduct example
definition: Leading others to believe you're still registered when you're not.

term: Cross-licensing
definition: lets both patentees use each other's inventions legally when one patents an improvement to the other's invention.

term: Consideration
definition: Any amendment must be backed by new consideration (something of value exchanged).

term: What is a tort?
definition:
A civil wrong where someone’s actions (or carelessness) cause harm to another person, and the injured person can sue for compensation instead of criminal punishment.

A tort happens when one person hurts another person or their property, even if it wasn’t on purpose.
It’s not a crime, so no one goes to jail — but the injured person can sue for money to make up for their loss.
Torts cover things like accidents (negligence), defamation, or trespassing.
It’s part of civil law, not criminal law.
In short: a tort means “you hurt me — now fix it with money.”

term: What is a letter of intent (LOI)?
definition:
short note showing intent to sign a full contract later.

A letter of intent is like saying, “I’m serious about this deal, but we’re not signing the final contract yet.”
It shows someone’s interest and commitment, but without all the final details locked in.
It’s often used in business, jobs, or big projects to start things off.
Think of it like a promise to keep talking and move forward.
It’s not legally binding (usually), but it shows you’re not just window-shopping.
It helps get everyone on the same page before the real contract.

term: Moral rights
definition: personal, non-economic creator rights; right to be credited & to protect the work's integrity.

term: Industrial-design application
definition: needs name, fee, drawings; a descriptive statement of unique features is optional.

term: Expert witness
definition: Help judge/jury understand technical issues and add credibility (expert opinion is admissible).

term: Movable tangible personal property
definition: Chattels.

term: What is a fundamental breach, and what can the innocent party do?
definition: A fundamental breach goes to the root of the contract and denies most/all benefits; the innocent party may treat the contract as terminated and sue for damages.

term: Usual split of content in Canadian Acts & Regulations
definition: Act = purpose & principles; Regulation = detailed rules for applying the Act.

term: Disability-based discrimination
definition: Can be allowed if the job genuinely cannot be done with the disability (a 'bona fide occupational requirement').

term: Why 'gelato' is un-registrable as a Canadian trademark
definition: It's a descriptive foreign word for the goods themselves; such marks are refused.

term: Obligations in a performance bond
definition: Primary obligations / Secondary obligations.

term: After ADR uncovers professional misconduct
definition:
ADR stops and a formal complaint goes to the regulator to protect the public.

If ADR finds signs of professional misconduct, the process stops right away.
Instead of continuing the dispute, a formal complaint is sent to the regulator.
This is done to protect the public from harm and hold the professional accountable.

Example

During a mediation, a client reveals their accountant faked tax documents.
Because that’s professional misconduct, the ADR process ends.
A complaint is filed with the accounting board.
The board investigates and may suspend the accountant’s license.
This step puts public safety before finishing the private dispute.

term: Allowed ways to end a contract after contractor's fundamental breach
definition:
(1) Keep contractor & sue for damages or (2) Terminate & sue for damages.

If a contractor seriously breaks the contract (called a fundamental breach), you have two options:

Continue with the contractor but ask the court for money to cover your losses.

End the contract completely and then go to court to get money for the damage.
These are the legal choices available when the contractor does something really wrong that ruins the deal.

Example:

Let’s say a contractor was hired to build a swimming pool, but they used the wrong materials and made it unsafe.
As the client, you could keep the contractor to fix it but ask for money for the problems.
Or, you could cancel the contract and sue them to pay for the damages and hire someone else.
Both ways are allowed if the mistake is big enough.
A small mistake wouldn’t count—it has to be serious.
This protects you if the contractor really messes up.

term:
Which of the following is NOT a role performed by technical societies in Canada?

A) Providing technical resources such as journals, conferences, and training
B) Supporting professional development and networking
C) Developing technical standards in specialized fields
D) Licensing engineers and geoscientists to legally practice
definition:
D) Licensing engineers and geoscientists to legally practice

When we talk about technical societies (like IEEE, ASME, CSCE, CIM, etc.), their job is to support the technical growth of professionals. They publish journals, host conferences, set technical standards, and help engineers learn new things.

But they do NOT have any legal power.

They cannot:

• Give you a licence
• Allow you to legally call yourself P.Eng. or P.Geo.
• Discipline you for unethical behaviour
• Give you permission to practise engineering or geoscience

Those powers belong only to provincial/territorial regulators (like APEGA, EGBC, PEO, Engineers Geoscientists Manitoba, etc.). Regulators exist because engineering and geoscience affect public safety, so the government gives these regulators legal authority.

Think of it this way, Nima:
A technical society is like a gym — a place to train, practice, connect, and get better.
A regulator is like the government licensing office — the place that decides who is legally allowed to drive.

term: Historic event after which Canadian technical societies multiplied rapidly
definition: After World War II - the post-war boom in engineers/geoscientists sparked many new societies.

term: Can you directly join the Canadian Federation of Engineering Students (CFES)?
definition: You cannot join the federation directly; join one of its member societies and you're automatically included.

term: What is a federation?
definition:
A federation is like an umbrella that brings together several separate societies so they can speak with one big voice.

Example: The Canadian Federation of Earth Sciences unites various provincial geoscience societies under one national group to tackle issues like funding and advocacy together.

term: Use of supervisor's seal
definition: No - only the P.Eng. who owns the seal may sign & seal.

term: Unauthorized practice
definition: Verbally claiming to be an engineer after licence expiry = unauthorized practice.

term: Bylaws
definition: Internal operating rules of the association.

term: What are the main services provided by technical societies?
definition: They publish journals, codes, and standards, and offer networking opportunities.

term: Name the two Canadian jurisdictions where geoscience remains unregulated.
definition: Yukon & Prince Edward Island.

term: Agreement on Internal Trade (AIT)
definition: Trade agreement that lets a licensed P.Eng. move from NS to Alberta (or vice-versa) with minimal paperwork.

term: Acceptable title for 2-yr Member-/Engineer-in-Training
definition: "Senior Technical Analyst" - avoids the protected word Engineer.

term: What is the legal status of copyright protection in Canada - automatic or requiring registration?
definition: Protection is AUTOMATIC the moment your original work is fixed in a material form (like saved as a file, written down, recorded). Registration is optional, but it provides a formal certificate and legal presumption of ownership, which is valuable proof in court.

term: Public disclosure and patents
definition: Any pre-filing public disclosure can bar patentability.

term: What does “per diem” pay mean, and when is it useful?
definition:
Per diem = paid by a daily rate. It’s useful when the total length of the job is uncertain, so workers are fairly paid for each day they work.

(Example

A consultant is hired for a project with no set end date.
Instead of guessing the total cost, the company offers $400 per diem.
That means the consultant gets $400 each day they work.
If they work 3 days, they earn $1,200.
This method keeps things simple while the project details are still unclear.
It’s like saying, “We’ll pay you daily while we figure things out.)

term: Who holds the actual legal power to grant a P.Eng. or P.Geo. license?
definition:
The Rule: ONLY provincial or territorial associations (e.g., Engineers Geoscientists Manitoba - EGM) have the legal authority to issue a P.Eng. or P.Geo. license.

The Trap: Technical societies (like ASME, IEEE, or SAE) CANNOT grant legal licenses. They only offer memberships, networking, or technical certifications. A certificate from a technical society does not give you the legal right to stamp engineering drawings.

term: Discipline-oriented society
definition: A society focused on a single profession, organized by field rather than region.

term: Sealing a drawing
definition: Both engineers must stamp; each writes their area of responsibility beside their seal.

term: Core licensing criteria
definition: 1. Accredited degree 2. 4 yrs qualifying experience 3. Law-&-ethics exam (NPPE); many need a good-character check.

term: In Canada, how long does a registered trademark last, and what are the renewal terms (post-2019)?
definition: 10 years from registration; renewable every 10 years thereafter.

term: Public disclosure that can bar patents
definition: Advertising your invention before filing counts as public disclosure and can bar a patent.

term: Most-important criterion in work-experience assessment
definition: Application of theory is rated highest.

term: EIT signs 'P.Eng.'
definition: Could face Enforcement (or Discipline if EITs are classified as members) for illegal title use.

term: Persian translation of liable
definition: مسئول، پاسخگو ( بار منفی داره )

term: Persian translation of Client
definition: کارفرما (کسی که برای یک پروژه مهندس هایر می کنه)

term: Persian translation of "paying dues"
definition: پرداخت حق عضویت

term:
Which of the following is NOT typically a principle found in most engineering and geoscience Association Codes of Ethics?
A) Stay informed and continually develop one's knowledge
B) Ensure appropriate and adequate compensation for engineering work
C) Participate in Association voting activities
D) Hold paramount the safety of the public
definition: C) Participate in Association voting activities

term:
In Canada, how is the professional status and practice of engineers primarily regulated?
A) Self-regulated
B) Regulated by incentives
C) Regulated by the government
D) Deregulated
definition: A) Self-regulated

term: Why was National Instrument 43-101 created after the Bre-X mining scandal?
definition: To prevent false or misleading claims about mineral discoveries.

term: The purpose of a Letter of Assurance is to confirm that the construction was completed in accordance with the ____________ and ____________ signed and sealed by the professional.
definition: plans / specifications

term:
Which of the following is *NOT* a valid way for a professional to fulfill their duty to themselves?
a) Insist on adequate payment from your client or employer.
b) Insist that you are awarded all rights under the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
c) Insist that your best interest is a priority.
d) Insist that you are provided with a satisfactory work environment.
definition: c) Insist that your best interest is a priority.

term:
According to most Occupational Health and Safety Acts, who is responsible for *supplying and who is responsible for using* personal protective equipment (PPE)?
a) Employer supplies / Worker uses
b) Supervisor supplies / Worker uses
c) Worker supplies / Employer uses
d) Provincial association supplies / Employee uses
definition: a) Employer supplies / Worker uses

term:
The "cradle-to-grave" approach in hazard assessment considers:
a) Worst-case scenarios and their likelihood
b) Whether construction standards will become outdated
c) Hazards across all age groups in the affected population
d) All potential hazards from construction through to decommissioning
definition: d) All potential hazards from construction through to decommissioning

term:
In a construction project involving multiple contractors, the individual responsible for coordinating safety and ensuring the protection of both employees and the public is called the ___________.
a) health and safety coordinator
b) prime contractor
c) project lead
d) director of public safety
definition: b) prime contractor

term:
In a construction project, the individual specified in the contract as responsible for safety is called the __________, and is responsible for the safety of __________.
a) health and safety coordinator / employees and the public
b) prime contractor / employees and the public
c) project lead / employees only
d) director of public safety / the public only
definition: b) prime contractor / employees and the public

term:
What is the common calculation used to determine the Factor of Safety in engineering design?
a) Ratio of benefits to risks to society
b) Ratio of load capacity to expected load on a structure or system
c) Total project cost plus insurance
d) Added safety cost plus damage insurance

term: What is the legal concept of "legal precedent" or "court precedent"?

Answer: رویه قضایی (roviyé ghazâ'i)

Summary: A sequence of previous court decisions that serve as a guide or rule for future cases involving similar facts or legal issues.

Professional Context: As a professional, understanding legal precedent is crucial. Over time, courts interpret laws (such as environmental regulations or workplace safety acts). These established interpretations (precedents) *define* the legal standards you are expected to uphold in your practice, sometimes even beyond the literal text of the statutes. Knowing significant precedents helps ensure compliance and avoid liability.

term: What must an engineer do when reviewing another engineer's work before sealing it?

term: What were some causes of the 1907 and 1916 bridge disasters?

term: In Callum's fraud case, can he use double jeopardy to block civil claims?
definition: No. It doesn't apply to civil claims.

term: What defense stops a second criminal prosecution for the same crime?
definition: Double jeopardy.

term: Double jeopardy does not block which actions?
definition:
civil

(Criminal case = government tries to punish (jail/fine).

Civil case = a person/business wants compensation (money) or a court order.
Double jeopardy only blocks the criminal do-over. The civil lane stays open.

Example: You’re found not guilty of careless driving in criminal court. The injured driver can still sue you for damages in civil court. That lawsuit is not blocked.)

term: What kinds of consequences can follow a criminal conviction besides jail?
definition:
Heavy fines, civil damages, and license revocation. (License REVOCATION is the most important one!)

If you're convicted of a crime, jail isn’t the only consequence.
You might also get big fines, be forced to pay money to victims, or even lose your professional license.
License revocation is the most serious for professionals—it means you can’t legally work in your field anymore.
So, a criminal record can ruin your career, not just your freedom.
These extra penalties are often long-term and life-changing.

3) Example

Say an engineer is convicted of fraud in a project.
They might get fined $50,000, and the client could sue for civil damages.
But the worst part? Their engineering license gets revoked.
Now they can’t sign or work on engineering projects again.
Even after jail or fines, that career damage lasts forever.
So, one conviction can lead to multiple serious consequences.

term: When would a member use the Code of Ethics?
definition: In complex or unclear professional situations.

term: What is the Persian term for “obligation (legal/professional duty)” and give a simple example.
definition: تعهد (ta‘ahhod). Example: «مهندسان تعهد دارند که از مردم محافظت کنند.»

term: Level ___ is the first level where you can work without supervision.
definition: Level C - Professional Engineer

term: What are your two obligations when you identify a hazard on a project?

term: The ________ is the amount you pay before the insurer covers a claim.

term: When may an engineer apply their seal to a document?

definition: No; each discipline and the approving professional must seal.

term: Why does the overruling engineer seal the document in an overruling situation?
